epigraph.text:
But those who hope in the Lord will renew their 
strength. They will soar on wings like eagles; they will run and not 
grow weary.

content.text:
A rate is a special type of ratio that compares quantities with different 
units. Rates are essential for describing motion, pricing, productivity, 
and countless other real-world situations.

content.title: Rate
content.content:
A **rate** is a ratio that compares two quantities measured in 
different units. Rates are often expressed using the word "per," 
meaning "for each" or "for every."

content.title: Identifying rates
content.problem:
Which of the following are rates?

1. 60 miles per hour

2. 3 boys to 4 girls

3. \$2.50 per pound

4. 5 pencils for 3 erasers
content.solution:
1. **Rate** (miles and hours are different units)

2. **Ratio** (boys and girls are the same type of thing—people)

3. **Rate** (dollars and pounds are different units)

4. **Ratio** (pencils and erasers are both objects, often 
 considered a ratio)

content.text:
A unit rate is a rate with a denominator of 1. Unit rates are especially 
useful for comparing different options.

content.title: Unit Rate
content.content:
A **unit rate** is a rate in which the second quantity (the 
denominator) is 1 unit. We find unit rates by dividing.

content.title: Finding a Unit Rate
content.content:
Divide the first quantity by the second quantity to get the rate 
"per one" of the second unit.

content.title: Finding a unit rate
content.problem:
A car travels 280 miles on 8 gallons of gas. Find the unit rate in 
miles per gallon.
content.solution:
Divide miles by gallons:
\[
\frac{280 \text{ miles}}{8 \text{ gallons}} = 35 \text{ miles per gallon}
\]
The car gets $\boxed{35 \text{ miles per gallon}}$.

content.title: Finding a unit price
content.problem: A 12-pack of soda costs \$5.40. What is the price per can?
content.solution:
Divide total price by number of cans:
\[
\frac{\$5.40}{12 \text{ cans}} = \$0.45 \text{ per can}
\]
The unit price is $\boxed{\$0.45 per can}$.

content.text: Unit rates help us make fair comparisons between different options.

content.title: Comparing prices
content.problem:
Which is the better buy?

- Store A: 15 oz of cereal for \$4.50
 \item Store B: 20 oz of cereal for \$5.60
content.solution:
Find the unit price (price per ounce) for each:

- Store A: $\frac{\$4.50}{15 oz} = \$0.30$ per oz

- Store B: $\frac{\$5.60}{20 oz} = \$0.28$ per oz

$\$0.28 < \$0.30$, so $\boxed{\text{Store B}}$ is the better buy.

content.title: Comparing speeds
content.problem:
Runner A covers 4 miles in 32 minutes. Runner B covers 5 miles in 45 
minutes. Who is faster?
content.solution:
Find each runner's pace (minutes per mile):

- Runner A: $\frac{32 \text{ min}}{4 \text{ mi}} = 8$ min per mile

- Runner B: $\frac{45 \text{ min}}{5 \text{ mi}} = 9$ min per mile

A lower time per mile is faster, so $\boxed{\text{Runner A}}$ is faster.

content.title: Comparing generosity
content.problem:
Church A has 200 members and donated \$15,000 to missions. Church B has 
150 members and donated \$12,000 to missions. Which church gave more 
per member?
content.solution:
Find the giving per member:

- Church A: $\frac{\$15,000}{200} = \$75$ per member

- Church B: $\frac{\$12,000}{150} = \$80$ per member

$\boxed{\text{Church B}}$ gave more per member.

content.text:
Speed is one of the most common rates. The relationship between distance, 
rate (speed), and time is fundamental.

content.title: Finding distance
content.problem: A car travels at 55 miles per hour for 3 hours. How far does it travel?

content.title: Finding time
content.problem:
A plane travels 1,800 miles at 450 miles per hour. How long does the 
flight take?

content.title: Finding rate
content.problem:
A cyclist rides 42 miles in 3.5 hours. What is the cyclist's average 
speed?
content.solution:
\[
\text{Rate} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{42}{3.5} = 12 \text{ mph}
\]
The cyclist's average speed is $\boxed{12 \text{ mph}}$.

content.text: Rates appear in many contexts beyond speed.

content.title: Rate of work
content.problem: A machine produces 240 widgets in 8 hours. What is the production rate?
content.solution:
\[
\frac{240 \text{ widgets}}{8 \text{ hours}} = 30 \text{ widgets per hour}
\]
The machine produces $\boxed{30 \text{ widgets per hour}}$.

content.title: Population density
content.problem:
A city has 450,000 people living in 90 square miles. What is the 
population density?
content.solution:
\[
\frac{450,000 \text{ people}}{90 \text{ sq mi}} = 5,000 \text{ people per square mile}
\]
The population density is $\boxed{5,000 \text{ people per sq mi}}$.

battle to the strong... but time and chance happen to them all.

has written the universe.
